blocks:
Ayahs

أَلْهَىٰكُمُ ٱلتَّكَاثُرُ (١)
Rivalry [and vainglory] distracted you (1)
حَتَّىٰ زُرْتُمُ ٱلْمَقَابِرَ (٢)
until you visited [even] the graves. (2)
كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٣)
No indeed, soon you will know! (3)
ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٤)
No indeed, soon you will know for yet another time! (4)
كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٥)
Indeed, were you to know with certain knowledge, (5)
لَتَرَوُنَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ (٦)
you would have surely seen hell [in this very life]. (6)
ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّهَا عَيْنَ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٧)
Afterward you will surely see it with the eye of certainty. (7)
ثُمَّ لَتُسْـَٔلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٨)
Then, on that day, you will surely be questioned concerning the Blessing. (8)

Content and Virtue of Surah "At-Takathur"

Many commentators believe that this Surah was revealed in "Mecca." Therefore, the discourse regarding boasting mentioned within it primarily pertains to the tribes of the Quraysh, who used to pride themselves and boast to one another over illusory matters.

However, some—such as the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan—believe it was revealed in Medina, and that the boasting mentioned refers to the Jews or two tribes of the Ansar. Nevertheless, the Meccan origin of the Surah seems more accurate given its strong resemblance to other Meccan Surahs.

The overall content of this Surah first censures and blames those who boast to one another based on illusory matters. It then gives a warning regarding the issue of the Hereafter, the Resurrection, and the fire of Hell, and finally issues a warning concerning the questioning and interrogation regarding blessings (ni'am).

The name of this Surah is derived from its first verse.

Regarding the virtue of its recitation, we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites it, Allah will not call him to account for the blessings He bestowed upon him in the abode of the world, and He will grant him a reward as if he had recited a thousand verses of the Quran." [1]

And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that reciting this Surah in obligatory and supererogatory prayers has the reward of the martyrdom of martyrs. [1] It is obvious that all this reward is for the one who recites it, implements it in his life's program, and harmonizes his soul and spirit with it.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 532. [1]: Ibid. (summarized).

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ (1) حَتَّى زُرْتُمُ الْمَقابِرَ (2) كَلاَّ سوف تَعْلَمُونَ (3) ثُمَّ كَلاَّ سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (4) كَلاَّ لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ (5) لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ (6) ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ (7) ثُمَّ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ (8)

Occasion of Revelation

As we have indicated, commentators believe that this Surah was revealed concerning tribes who boasted to one another, pridefully vaunting their large numbers, population, or wealth and property—to the extent that to increase the recorded numbers of their tribe, they would go to the graveyards and count the graves of each tribe's dead!

However, some consider it to refer to two tribes of the Quraysh in Mecca, some to two tribes of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Ansar in Medina, and some to the boasting of the Jews over others. Although its Meccan origin seems more correct.

Yet, it is certain that whatever these occasions of revelation may be, they never limit the general meaning of the verse.

Commentary

The Scourge of Rivalry and Boasting!

In these verses, it first says in a reproachful tone: "Competition in [worldly] increase and boasting to one another diverted you [from Allah and the Hereafter]" (أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ).

"Until you visited the graveyards [to count the dead]" (حَتَّى زُرْتُمُ الْمَقابِرَ). Another possibility suggested in the interpretation of the verse is that "rivalry" and "boasting" occupied them so much that it continued until the moment they entered the grave.

However, the first meaning is more consistent with the expression "زُرْتُمُ الْمَقابِرَ" (you visited the graves), as well as the occasions of revelation and the sermon in Nahj al-Balagha which, God willing, will be referred to later.

"Alhakum" is derived from the root "lahw," meaning to be preoccupied with trivial matters and to remain heedless of important goals and tasks. Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Lahw" is something that occupies a person and distracts him from his purposes and goals.

"Takathur" is from the root "kithrah," meaning to boast, vaunt, and show off to one another. [1] "Zurtum" is from the root "ziyarah" and "zawr" (rhyming with qawl), which originally means the upper part of the chest, then it came to be used for meeting and facing someone.

[1]: In everyday Persian usage, "Takathur" is used to mean the accumulation of wealth, whereas this meaning is not in its linguistic root; however, in some narrations that we will mention later, such a usage has appeared.

"Zawr" (rhyming with qamar) means the slanting of the upper part of the chest, and since a lie is a kind of deviation from the truth, it is called "zur" (rhyming with nur).

"Maqabir" is the plural of "maqbarah," meaning the place of a person's grave. Visiting the graves here is either a metaphor for death (according to some interpretations) or it means going to the graves to count them for the sake of boasting (according to the famous interpretation).

As we said, the second meaning seems more correct, and one of the evidences for it is the words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in Nahj al-Balagha regarding this matter. After reciting "أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ حَتَّى زُرْتُمُ الْمَقابِرَ," he said:

"How distant is the goal? How heedless are the visitors? And how disgraceful and terrible is the pride? They have remembered the decayed bones of those who have been dust for years—and what a remembrance! From such a far distance, they remember those who can neither benefit them. Do they boast over the ruins of their fathers? Or do they seek to be many by counting the dead and the non-existent? They seek the return of bodies whose fibers have disintegrated and whose movements have turned to stillness. These decayed bodies are more deserving of being a lesson than being a cause for pride!" [1]

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 221.

This sermon, of which we have only mentioned a part here, is so soul-shaking, eloquent, and explicit that Ibn Abi al-Hadid al-Mu'tazili says: "I swear by the One whom all nations swear by, that for fifty years until now, I have read this sermon more than a thousand times, and each time a new tremor, fear, and admonition has arisen within my heart, affecting my soul severely and making my limbs tremble. Never have I reflected upon it without thinking of the death of my family, relatives, and friends, vividly imagining that I am the very person the Imam عليه السلام has described."

"How many preachers, orators, and eloquent speakers have spoken on this subject, and I have listened and scrutinized their words, yet in none have I found the impact of the Imam's عليه السلام words!"

"This impact that his words leave on my heart either stems from the faith belonging to the speaker, or his intention of certainty and sincerity has caused it to penetrate souls and settle in hearts in this way." [1] In another part of his commentary, he says: "It would be fitting if all the eloquent speakers of the Arabs gathered in one assembly and this sermon were recited to them, that they should prostrate before it!" Here he refers to Mu'awiyah's statement regarding the eloquence of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام: "By Allah, no one established the path of eloquence for the Quraysh other than him!"

[1]: Sharh Nahj al-Balagha by Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Vol. 11, p. 153.

In the next verse, He threatens them severely with this statement, saying: "It is not as you imagine and boast about; you shall soon know the result of this illusory boasting" (كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ).

Again, for emphasis, He adds: "Then it is not as you imagine; you shall soon know" (ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ). A group of commentators considers these two verses to be a repetition and emphasis of a single point, both cryptically informing of the punishments awaiting these boastful, arrogant people.

While others consider the first to refer to the punishment of the grave and Barzakh (the intermediate realm) which man faces after death, and the second to refer to the punishment of the Resurrection.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is mentioned that he said: "We continued to doubt the punishment of the grave until 'أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ' was revealed, up to the point where He said: 'كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ'—He means in the grave—'ثُمَّ كَلَّا سَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ'—after the Resurrection." [1] In the Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr Razi, this matter is narrated from one of Ali's عليه السلام companions named Zur bin Hubaysh, who says: "We were in doubt about the punishment of the grave until we heard from Ali عليه السلام that he said: 'This verse is proof of the punishment of the grave.'" [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 534. [1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 32, p. 78.

Then He adds: "It is not as you boasters imagine; if you had faith in the Hereafter and knew it with the knowledge of certainty (ilm al-yaqin), you would never have pursued these matters and would not have engaged in this vain boasting and rivalry" (كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ). [2]

Again, for further emphasis and warning, He adds: "You shall certainly see Hell" (لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ).

"Then, upon entering it, you shall see it with the eye of certainty (ayn al-yaqin)" (ثُمَّ لَتَرَوُنَّها عَيْنَ الْيَقِينِ).

"Then, on that Day, you shall definitely be questioned about the blessings you had" (ثُمَّ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ).

On that Day, you must clarify in what way you spent these God-given blessings. Did you use them for divine obedience or for His disobedience? Or did you waste the blessings and never fulfill their rights?

[2]: Some believe that the word "Kalla" in such cases is for emphasis and means "Truly." Tabarsi narrates this in Majma' al-Bayan and says: "The Arabs emphasize with Kalla and Haqqan."

Key Points

1. The Source of Boasting and Vainglory

From the above verses, it is understood that one of the main factors of boasting, rivalry, and vainglory is ignorance regarding divine reward and punishment and a lack of faith in the Resurrection.

Furthermore, man's ignorance of his weaknesses and vulnerabilities, his beginning and his end, are other factors of this pride, arrogance, and boasting. For this reason, the Holy Quran, in order to break this boasting and rivalry, recounts the stories of past nations in various verses—how nations with vast resources and power were destroyed by simple means.

By the blowing of winds, by a heavenly spark (lightning), by an earthquake, by excessive rain—in short, by "water," "wind," "earth," and sometimes by "baked clay" (sijjil) and "small birds," they were destroyed and perished.

Despite this, what is all this boasting and pride for?!

Another factor for this is the feeling of weakness and inferiority resulting from failures, where individuals take refuge in boasting and vainglory to cover their failures. Hence, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"No man acts arrogantly or tyrannically except because of a humiliation he finds within himself." [1] Therefore, when he feels he has reached the level of perfection, he sees no need for this boasting.

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Three things are from the actions of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah): Boasting about lineage, slandering the honor and family reputation of others, and seeking rain through the stars." [1]

In another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Two things have destroyed people: Fear of poverty and seeking pride." [2] Truly, among the most important factors of greed, stinginess, worldliness, destructive rivalries, and many social vices are this groundless fear of poverty and the seeking of pride and superiority among individuals, tribes, and nations.

Hence, in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "I do not fear poverty for you, but I fear Takathur (rivalry in increase) for you." [3] "Takathur," as we mentioned before, originally means boasting, but sometimes it means greed and the accumulation of wealth, as we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Takathur is the gathering of wealth from unlawful sources, withholding it from its rightful dues, and locking it up in containers and chests." [1]

We end this extensive discussion with a meaningful Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In the commentary of "أَلْهاكُمُ التَّكاثُرُ," he said: "The son of Adam says: 'My wealth, my wealth!' while your wealth is only that which you eat and consume, or that which you wear and wear out, or that which you give in charity and send forth [for the Hereafter]!" [2]

This is a very interesting point: that every person's share of the vast wealth they collect—sometimes without the slightest care for its lawfulness or unlawfulness—is nothing but that small portion they eat, drink, wear, or give in the way of Allah. And we know: what a person consumes personally is negligible, so how much better it is to increase one's share through charity.

[1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 236, Chapter on Arrogance, Hadith 17. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 291. [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 290, Hadith 12. [3]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 387. [1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 662, Hadith 8. [2]: Sahih Muslim (as quoted in Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 534).

2. Certainty and Its Stages:

"Certainty" (Yaqin) is the opposite of "doubt," just as knowledge is the opposite of ignorance, and it means the clarity and fixity of something. According to what is understood from reports and narrations, the highest stage of faith is called "Certainty." Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام said:

"Faith is one degree higher than Islam, and piety (taqwa) is one degree higher than faith, and certainty is one degree superior to piety." Then he added: "And nothing less than certainty has been distributed among the people!"

The narrator asks: "What is certainty?" He says: "Trust in Allah, submission to Allah, satisfaction with Allah's decree, and entrusting all one's affairs to Allah!" [1] The superiority of the station of certainty over the stations of piety, faith, and Islam is something emphasized in other narrations as well. [2]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Among the signs of the correctness of a Muslim's certainty is that he does not please people by incurring the wrath of Allah, and he does not blame them for what Allah has not given him... Indeed, Allah, by His justice and equity, has placed spirit and comfort in certainty and satisfaction, and has placed anxiety and sorrow in doubt and discontent."

From these expressions and others, it is well understood that when a person reaches the station of certainty, a special tranquility encompasses his entire heart and soul.

Nevertheless, there are levels of certainty, which are referred to in the above verses and verse 95 of Surah Al-Waqi'ah (إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ), and they are three stages: [3]

  1. Knowledge of Certainty (Ilm al-Yaqin): This is when a person gains faith in something through various evidences, like one who gains faith in the existence of fire by observing smoke.
  2. Eye of Certainty (Ayn al-Yaqin): This is where a person reaches the stage of observation and sees the fire, for example, with his own "eye."
  3. Truth of Certainty (Haqq al-Yaqin): This is like one who enters the fire and feels its heat, becoming characterized by the attributes of fire; this is the highest stage of certainty.

Muhaqqiq Tusi says in one of his statements: "Certainty" is that firm, corresponding, and stable belief whose removal is impossible. In reality, it is composed of two knowledges: knowledge of the known, and knowledge that the contrary of that knowledge is impossible. It has several levels: Ilm al-Yaqin, Ayn al-Yaqin, and Haqq al-Yaqin. [1]

In reality, the first stage is general, the second stage is for the pious, and the third stage is specific to the elite and those drawn near (muqarrabun).

Hence, it is mentioned in a Hadith that it was said to the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "We have heard that some of the companions of Jesus used to walk on water!" He said: "If his certainty were stronger than that, he would have walked on air!"

The late "Allamah Tabataba'i," after mentioning this Hadith, adds: "Everything revolves around certainty in Allah the Sublimely Purified, and erasing the independence of the causes of the world of creation in their effects. Therefore, the more a person's belief and faith in the absolute power of God increases, the more the things of the world will become obedient and submissive to him." [2] This is the secret of the relationship between certainty and extraordinary control over the world of creation.

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 138, Hadith 4. [2]: Ibid., pp. 135-137. [3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 143. [1]: As quoted in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 143. [2]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 6, p. 200 (under verse 105 of Surah Al-Ma'idah).

3. Everyone Will Observe Hell!

The sentence "لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ" has two interpretations: First, that it refers to observing Hell in the Hereafter, which is specific to the disbelievers, or for all jinn and men, since according to some Quranic verses, everyone must pass by Hell.

Second, that it refers to observing it with spiritual witnessing in this very world. In this case, this sentence is the response to the conditional clause; He says: "If you had 'Knowledge of Certainty,' you would have observed 'Hell' in this very world with the eye of the heart," since we know that Paradise and Hell have already been created and have an external existence.

However, as we have previously indicated, the first interpretation is more consistent with the subsequent verses that speak of the Day of Resurrection. Therefore, it is a definitive and unconditional statement.

4. What Blessings Will Be Questioned on the Day of Resurrection?

In the last verse of this Surah, we read that surely all of you will be interrogated about blessings on the Day of Resurrection. Some have said this blessing refers to "health" and "leisure," some consider it "physical well-being" and "security," and some have considered all blessings to be included in the verse.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "The blessing (na'im) is fresh dates and cool water!"

While in another Hadith, we read that Abu Hanifa asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام about the interpretation of this verse. The Imam عليه السلام returned the question to him and said: "What is na'im in your opinion?" He replied: "It is food and cool water." The Imam said: "If Allah wished to keep you before Him on the Day of Resurrection to ask you about every morsel you ate and every sip you drank, you would have to stand there for a very long time!" He asked: "Then what is na'im?"

The Imam said: "We are the People of the House (Ahl al-Bayt) through whom Allah has blessed His servants, and through whom He created harmony among them after discord, and joined their hearts through us and made them brothers after they were enemies, and through us He guided them to Islam... Yes, the na'im is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and his family." [1]

The interpretation of these seemingly different narrations is that "na'im" has a very broad meaning that includes all divine favors, whether "spiritual" like religion, faith, Islam, the Quran, and Wilayah (guardianship), or various "material" blessings, whether individual or social. However, blessings that are of greater importance, such as the blessing of "faith and Wilayah," will be questioned more—whether their rights were fulfilled or not. Apparently, the narrations that exclude material blessings from the scope of this verse are pointing to the fact that you should not abandon the more important instances and turn to the smaller ones; in reality, it is a warning to people regarding the hierarchy of divine favors and blessings and that they have a heavy responsibility toward them.

And how is it possible not to be questioned about these blessings when they are great capitals placed at the disposal of man, and he must precisely appreciate each one, offer thanks for it, and spend it in its proper place?

O Allah! Make Your endless blessings, especially the blessing of faith and Wilayah, continuous for us. O Lord! Grant us the success to fulfill the rights of all these blessings. O God! Increase these great blessings and never take them away from us. Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah At-Takathur.

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 535.
